author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
There are multiple types of matchmaking (MM), the most known is SBMM (Skill-Based Matchmaking).
It ignores things like player level or total playtime and instead focuses purely on how good a player actually is (based on stats like accuracy, hs rate, kill to deaths ratio etc).
The goal is to prevent high-skill players from abusing new players or smurfing.
However, this type of matchmaking implies a higher number of active lobbies, which increases server costs and can lead to playerbase fragmentation.
It’s a very effective system to ensure fair matches between players of similar skill, but it requires a healthy population to avoid long queue times.
For now, I think we’re not quite there yet in terms of numbers — but I have no doubt we’ll catch up in the next few months.

In my opinion, the crew packages as rewards have been a genius move — they really motivate players to keep grinding.
I'd definitely keep marketing them on Twitter — it can boost engagement like crazy!

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Personally, I prefer the option to engage in SBMM, so that I can enjoy feeling somewhat competent.   Even now, sometimes I enter an arena and get constantly smoked. For some people, this may help them to get better.  For others, it's frustrating to always lose and they just give up.  I'd rather get good in the kitty pool and slowly move up to play against better players when I'm ready. But I do realize we need more DAU for that.

author: odvb
content: I think time to play (time to get into a match and play) is also a big deal, for example I think Apex does an amazing job with this. Getting into a game and dying right away is fun. We like to call it dropping hot but it's not as painful because while spectating those sweaties who killed you you're actively in matchmaking with sub 4 minute wait periods. People will also smurf (create a new account to get around SBMM) so I wonder if the game loop could be tighter? Or maybe they make it fun watching others while waiting to matchmake (sorry find a match). I love the idea of throwing tomatoes tbh. I also like the idea of jumping into a match spetacting some friends if I boot up the game later than expected.

author: beyondthehorizon2620Ustur
content: @Deleted User some thought on the Gun Game mode (quick review from a PC noob) I had a really fun time playing this morning even though I am AWFUL! Lol I love that you guys made it so you have to get two kills per weapon to progress! Here are my thoughts that might help newer players: when I first logged in, it was me and a few new people. The new people seemed confused and did not realize the Gatling Gun had a spool time. So I think they thought the game was broken. They also seemed confused when they got a new gun and I think they were trying to figure out how to switch guns. Maybe quick instructions pop up at some point when joining in? And for me, the spool time on Gatling Gun make it a really difficult weapon for a newbie. You have to get that timing just right. I am personally awful at it. Once I get the two kills and get a new gun, it was waaaaaay easier. Maybe Gatling should not be starter weapon. Later in the morning some elites joined the match after about an hour. Some of the top players. They came in and just Superman melee punched all us newbies to death over and over. I could see we all quit one by one. Including myself after three matches. It wasn’t fun at all just constantly getting punched to death while I’m so new and trying to figure out movement skills still. Maybe you should not get xp/progress unless you kill with the weapon you are assigned? Thanks for listening!!!

author: beyondthehorizon2620Ustur
content: Thanks for the feedback. Yeah Gatling gun has been notoriously difficult for us to balance. Will keep at it though. I think needing to spend a round or two to understand the game mode is ok though. It took me much longer to understand what I was doing in Off the Grid. To this day, I still can't do the gliding mechanic in their game correctly, I do feel our movement is more intuitive.   A focus of ours will certainly be how to improve the new player experience a bit. A common technique is to allow new players to play against AI for a bit before pitting them against actual players.

reply.preview: Thanks for the feedback. Yeah Gatling gun has been notoriously difficult for us to balance. Will keep at it though. content:
Just wanted to let team know that there started to be an expectation for connecting wallet and grinding XP from web3 content creators and players. (some kind of airdrop or prizes)

First saw it from "Inspector Mindblow - Tony; founder of Tokenize360 -" YT channel as a speculation  starting at 6:50 in the video.

Then today; HIMARS's tweet. 

Both are attached. 

Why sharing these? I have been in crypto for very long time. At first, speculations might be good for interactions and hype. However, at the end, if nothing happens, web3 players might be disappointed. 
If these expectations grow, more will be looking for an airdrop or smt. (other than 5 crew pack prizes) 

We might need to build our communication on better foundation so as to avoid future accidents.

@DominicVain

author: Matteo985xxxMUD
content:
I'm not sure if this idea has already been proposed or considered by the team. It’s still a rough concept, so forgive me if it’s not appropriate—but I think it might be worth exploring.

PROPOSAL: Future use of POLIS through user-created skins

Given that Star Atlas is also a development platform, imagine if users could design ship or character skins and sell them on the official marketplace. These skins could then be traded just like ships.
To list a skin for sale, the creator would need a certain amount of POLIS Voting Power (PvP).
Example: I want to sell a skin for $5 → I need 500 PvP (that is 100x the listing price). I can reach this by either:
buying 500 POLIS,,locking 50 POLIS for 5 years (approx. 500 PvP).,If my PvP drops below the required threshold, my skin is removed from the primary market (only secondary sales remain). If I want to keep my item listed on the primary market, I can restore my PvP either by adding more POLIS or by extending the lock duration. The PvP-to-price ratio (for exemple 100x) would be defined and adjusted by the DAO.

This system would:
support creators with a clear earning path,,reduce workload for the core team,,create new demand for POLIS staking,,generate marketplace fees for ATMTA.,
I'm curious to know if this idea could be implemented in the future, whether you find it interesting, and how it could be improved.

author: Matteo985xxxMUD
content: We do have a UGC skin crafting model, but it is coupled with ATLAS tokenomics, not POLIS. The concept consists of Skin Crafters either mining or purchasing commodity resources from the game in various crafting recipes. More exotic and rarer shaders/colors/etc. will come from higher risk zones in-game. Each recipe will have resource requirements defined per unit of skin crafted. These users would also benefit from participating in the ATLAS locker by reducing marketplace fees.

(And I don't mean this as a jab, but in a positive way. ).  Maybe next TH all this becomes moot, because we get stuff announced.

author: Definitely_Not_JayWalk
content: I'm not going to touch the PIP itself — I still haven’t fully made up my mind on it. I do see the reasoning behind it, and I understand that people have different aptitudes and try to contribute in different ways. No one should be dismissed for that. I just feel its a lot of money for the current state of DAO treasury though. We’re all trying to row in the same direction, even if through different means.  That said, Star Atlas is and will be a space game. But it’s impossible not to understand how development works: we’re "investing" a huge amount of time into building ships, for example, — which are the core of the game — and the same amount of time would likely be enough to build multiple CSS from scratch. The thing is, building blocks need to come before the full game.  The arena shooter isn’t wasted time. It’s a standalone piece that lets us have fun, test in -game rewards mechanism, and — most importantly — see and feel development progress. That feedback loop is essential, both for the devs and for us.  I respect your perspective, but I strongly disagree with the idea that ATMTA isn't building a space game. What we're witnessing is a development process that’s usually hidden from players or investors. I still remember when first GTA IV leaks came out and people complained how bad facial animations were or who bad quality was (and that was a pre alpha capture). We’re still years away from a pre-alpha of the full game. What we have right now is a vertical slice — an arena shooter — and a glimpse of what Galia will be.  Maybe your message was more focused on the PIP, but I felt the need to offer this different take regarding the dev process.

At most we needs characters that can walk around exploring their ships. We could have gotten that with UE5 out of the box mechanics. None of those mechanics help me fly a ship in space, discover new worlds and fight battles in space...which what this game originally was. No idea what it is now. In fact not sure it's a game any more. Now this project is just an conglomerate of random IP half baked thoughts. Let's keep it going by writing a book and building a movie.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content:
I'm just going off of proposals. Even the council election #1 with the most votes ever collected (587) had a result of 181M PVP out of the 547M total at the time. The most recent highest vote was for Ryden systems at (202 votes) and a total PVP of 107M out of 547M. Winning with 81M of those being yes.

Data breakdown of Ryden Systems vote,Extremely Low Overall Participation

Only 11.62M POLIS locked for voting out of 360M total supply (3.23%)
Just 202 voters participated in this governance decision
Even if we assume only 30% of supply is circulating (~108M), participation is still only 10.76%

Voting Power Multipliers (Time-Lock Effect)

Voting power ranges from 0.0006x to 10x based on lock duration
40% of voters chose maximum lock periods (9-10x multiplier)
29% chose medium locks (5-9x multiplier)
31% chose short locks (<5x multiplier)
This creates massive disparities where someone with less POLIS but longer lock can outvote someone with 10x more tokens

Token Distribution Among Voters

Whales (1M+ POLIS): Only 3 voters but control 35% of voting power
Large holders (100K-1M): 23 voters control 51% of voting power
Small holders (<1K POLIS): 50 voters (25% of participants) control only 0.08% of power

Governance Capture Risk: With only 3.23% of supply participating, the protocol is vulnerable to governance attacks. A coordinated group controlling just 2-3% of total supply could dominate decisions.
Not Representative: 202 voters deciding for a protocol with 360M token supply suggests either:

Massive token concentration in non-voting wallets (exchanges, treasury, team)
Extreme voter apathy
High barriers to participation


Time-Lock Plutocracy: The system heavily favors those willing to lock tokens for extended periods, creating a two-tier system where commitment time matters more than stake size.
Participation Paradox: YES votes came from 76.5% of locked POLIS but only 2.47% of total supply, meaning protocol changes are being decided by a tiny fraction of token holders.
Median vs Mean Gap:

Average POLIS per voter: 57,535
Median POLIS per voter: 7,021
This 8x difference shows extreme inequality even among participants

author: Lysimach | Make Polis Great [STAR]
content: I think incentivization comes from how a vote affects people. If we did a PIP where we said everyone would get thousands if not millions of more dollars into their bags. We will see the biggest vote yet. But if we have a PIP saying we want to market and fund books ... it's not our money and only those who care about what that truly means to the wellbeing of the DAO and it's funds will vote. You can sort of see where votes went with Jupiter when they rewarded people for voting (it sounded amazing) until you realize you have to be a whale to make anything worth your hours of reviewing (or you just vote yes or no because it didn't truly mattter -- ruining the whole purpose of voting)

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: I see quite a few people challenged by the math here. The DAO does produce income every month that would largely offset the costs of this proposal, beyond the existing treasury balance. The key caveat here is that it would require recurring selling of ATLAS to cover the balance beyond $480k USDC the DAO possesses, which, honestly, I hate to see. Because I think ATLAS has enormous upside. But there is also opportunity cost of that value just sitting in the DAO.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Do i think pure token voted weight is the best option out there?  no defenitely not,  i dont think many concepts are correct from the start and things will grow and evolve,  including how token voting / weight is done.   Realms for example has a option for Quadric Weighting, this includes a bit of democracy into the weighting,    I think it should be possible to improve upon our current implementation, just like all the rest of the parts you guys are developing.  I understand it will be hard battle to fight, as the current power holders would need to approve and limit their own powers, which is likely not gonna happen with the current top holders But nothing set in stone right? its all code and regulations that we can change or will change overtime

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content: Analysis of the reticle movements: By examining the images from the video, here is what I can observe: Fluid and precise movements: The reticle seems to track the targets very accurately, often aiming directly at the head or body of the enemies. This may indicate aim assist, especially if the movements are too perfect to be natural. Tracking moving targets: In several frames, the reticle appears to "stick" to the enemies, even when they move quickly. This is a classic sign of aim assist, which automatically adjusts the reticle to follow the target. Quick transitions: The player quickly switches from one target to another, with minimal adjustments. This could be due to excellent skill, but it may also be amplified by aim assist.

content:
Would something like this viable to lower the sell pressure of the tokens, or is it far to early for something like that?
 Proposal: Star Atlas Monthly Subscription Package – $12.99

Goal:
Increase recurring revenue for the team, give players great value, and reduce token sell pressure on the open market by offering POLIS and ATLAS directly to subscribers instead of selling them on exchanges.
Included in the Monthly Package:
Exclusive Crew / Ship Pack
A “mystery pack” that opens on purchase, containing either a themed crew member or a small ship component.
Rotating theme each month to encourage collecting.
POLIS & ATLAS Tokens
Predefined monthly amount of each token.
By selling directly to players instead of on exchanges, the tokens bypass public sell walls and enter circulation more gradually.
Exclusive Monthly Skin or Pet  (Optional extra)
Unique cosmetic item only available during that month’s subscription.
Could be tied to in-game events, seasons, or lore.
Subscriber Perks (Optional extra)
Early access to some events or playtests.
enhanced discord features

Benefits to Star Atlas:
Steady Recurring Revenue: Predictable cash flow to support development.
Token Market Health: Tokens are sold directly to players who value them for in-game use, reducing immediate sell pressure.
Player Engagement: Exclusive skins/pets/crew encourage continued subscriptions and collection culture.
Lore & Community Tie-In: Monthly items could connect to ongoing story arcs, fueling engagement across media and events.

Example Pricing: $12.99 USD / month
(Balance of token amounts, crew/pack rarity, and cosmetics to be determined by the team.)
No Pay-to-Win Commitment:
All items are cosmetic or optional collectibles.
No combat advantages, stat boosts, or exclusive gameplay power.
Subscriptions should enhance player expression and community pride, not alter competitive balance.

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
I played the Battlefield beta last night. Personally, I’ve always preferred first-person shooters, first person has always been my favorite, we can say I was positively biased.

It felt frustrating, static, complex, and slow… I know SA still needs time to polish, but I would NEVER trade our movement system for any other game’s!

Sure, we could refine it a bit to make it even more fluid, but right now our movement system has no equal.

A few examples of how I’d slightly tweak it, for instance:
-Increase air strafing
-Increase max stamina to allow more flying
-Greatly reduce slide stamina cost to make slide-jumping more viable and bunny-hopping more frequent
-Allow lateral slides

None of these changes would ever be possible in a “top tier” game like Battlefield, since their movement system is at a “turtle level” compared to ours!

author: 《VΛ》 KATIREcon Vantarah
content: We shouldn't send signals to players that ATMTA will bail them out. It disrupts the economy.  If you reward players for making bad decisions this time, what will happen next time?  The people who made Quantum Nodes this time, will stop, and they will choose the easy route, e.g. fic1s, fic2s, and Starpaths.   And then the economy would have ground to a halt because FICs would never refill.  Taken a step further ATMTA would be in an even worse position because they would have to invalidate everyone FICs or cave in and reward bad gameplay, causing further deterioration of the system.  There is plenty of time to switch to different FICs if you are worried about backlog.  I would also encourage any players in MUD or USTUR to come play on Oni.  We have 3-4 more refreshes on both factions and are gearing up for additional 5th refresh on MUD.  FIC2s are in demand and lasted about 7 days this time.  Come to Oni and be rewarded.

reply.preview: We shouldn't send signals to players that ATMTA will bail them out. It disrupts the economy. If you reward players for making bad decisions this time, what will happen next time? The people who made Quantum Nodes this time, will stop, and they will choose the easy route, e.g. fic1s, fic2s, and Starpaths. And then the economy would have ground to a halt because FICs would never refill. Taken a step further ATMTA would be in an even worse position because they would have to invalidate everyone FICs or cave in and reward bad gameplay, causing further deterioration of the system. There is plenty of time to switch to different FICs if you are worried about backlog. I would also encourage any players in MUD or USTUR to come play on Oni. We have 3-4 more refreshes on both factions and are gearing up for additional 5th refresh on MUD. FIC2s are in demand and lasted about 7 days this time. Come to Oni and be rewarded.

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
I’m trying to calm down some heated spirits (who haven’t seen the Town Hall). Can I make a couple of statements so you can confirm if I’ve got them right, or correct me if not?

-Only SAGE will be migrated to Zink; other programs will remain on Solana.

-POLIS will stay exclusively on Solana. SPL tokens (like R4s, raw materials etc.) will exist only on Zink, while ATLAS will coexist on both chains with a bridge to move it.

-Will people be able to purchase Zink token to pay fees? How? (I’m thinking about new players who start from scratch.)

-Will Zink be less safe than Solana? (I don’t think so, since with the trilemma problem you can focus on safety and scalability at the same time with no issues, but I’d like give them the team’s reply.)

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content: Initially yes. And probably an extended timeline before we move any other core systems like Marketplace and the DAO over to zink, if at all. We don't want to fully separate from Solana. That's where the current network effect resides. And the liquidity., Not necessarily. Again, depends on if/when we move other systems over to zink. But initially, it'll only be SA game systems starting with C4, (knock on wood) we've never had a major security vulnerability. We'll be tight on managing the network, and we do have several third-party validators coming online for launch. So it's still decentralized, just less so than Solana for example. As the network further decentralizes, security increases.,

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content: Z.ink should be just as safe as Solana since it's a copy of Solana. But there is still dangers to be aware of which I'm sure team will mitigate as best as possible.  z.inc could possibly lag behind Solana mainnet versions.,team could modify z.inc and introduce errors (swagner said team will not be modifying consensus,bridging is generally a higher failure point, but has come a long way,smaller validator set than Solana, however nakamoto coefficient likely to be similar so zinc is still in a strong position.,

author: Kr1gs
content: Yeah, ok I see what you mean. We've talked a bit about what direction to go next with transportation. Another idea we've been considering  revolves around the starbase objectives dictating how transport fleets are assigned.   For example, you set inventory targets for your bases through a starbase manifest, and give them a priority  rank (and not set the fleet specifically to a task), and then any fleets you have divide their time to accomplish your various starbase tasks.   But yeah, your approach is a logical next step if we continue to enhance fleet logistics.   The nice thing about Holosim is we can try new game mechanics from chapter to chapter until we land on something everyone loves.   Chapter 1 is very much about promoting Star Atlas with help from Solflare and a number of content creators, so the 5 week timeline was very much about syncing up all those efforts.   In the future we can do longer chapters with different objectives or very short chapters to test specific scenarios.  No matter what we are doing, some version of Holosim should be available for people to onboard into Star Atlas, and be a tool you can introduce new players to SA without any financial commitment.

reply.preview: Yeah, ok I see what you mean. We've talked a bit about what direction to go next with transportation. Another idea we've been considering revolves around the starbase objectives dictating how transport fleets are assigned. For example, you set inventory targets for your bases through a starbase manifest, and give them a priority rank (and not set the fleet specifically to a task), and then any fleets you have divide their time to accomplish your various starbase tasks. But yeah, your approach is a logical next step if we continue to enhance fleet logistics. The nice thing about Holosim is we can try new game mechanics from chapter to chapter until we land on something everyone loves. Chapter 1 is very much about promoting Star Atlas with help from Solflare and a number of content creators, so the 5 week timeline was very much about syncing up all those efforts. In the future we can do longer chapters with different objectives or very short chapters to test specific scenarios. No matter what we are doing, some version of Holosim should be available for people to onboard into Star Atlas, and be a tool you can introduce new players to SA without any financial commitment.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I hear you Siggy. And that was my initial intention when we first introduced "Apollo." However, having discussed this extensively internally, there was simply no path forward using ATLAS or POLIS on the network to achieve broad decentralization and break outside of the existing audience for Star Atlas. We need a token allocation to incentivize growth in the network and actually achieve decentralization of the L1 -- how do we pay validators if there is no incentive pool to do so? Our options were to either take supply from ATLAS and POLIS, mint more and dilute our existing holders, or launch with a new token we could use for network incentives.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: It kind of is this, btw. I didn't go deep into this on the call yesterday, but I think of z.ink as the substrate of the metaverse. Star Atlas is the first 3D manifestation of that metaverse concept, and there will be plenty of opportunities to bring other applications that build on z.ink, into Star Atlas. We're already working on this from the partnership angle. But simple example, imagine a Raydium or Jup trading floor with a commercial storefront inside Star Atlas. That doesn't necessarily require z.ink, but we can prioritize other teams who are building on z.ink for those types of opportunities. Helping us grow z.ink, and in turn, helping us grow Star Atlas.

author: [Λ] Neo_AArmstrong [EC]Econ Vantarah
content:
I'm just extremely against F2P modules offering fixed rewards for reaching a certain progression point, even if the reward is the smallest possible (for example, if by Season 15 of Holosim a reward were introduced where all players who complete the main quest arc receive a Fimbul Airbike and a Crew).
I don't think this is even a possibility for Holosim, but I'm already outlining my opinion because I believe that if this were to happen in the future, it would be extremely detrimental to the economy.
Let me explain:

In Brazil, we call them "cousins."
Imagine that a player with many "cousins" decides to join Holosim and, if they reach a certain level, they earn 1 Fimbul Airbike and 1 crew, extremely low rewards for a single player.
Now consider that this player has a very large family, 50,000 cousins to be exact.
This "player" alone would be the size of several whales we have today.
Now, multiply that by 500 people doing the same thing, and you reach the collapse of the economy, giving 1 Fimbul Airbike and 1 crew as a fixed reward for reaching a milestone in Holosim.

I would very much like to believe in the innocence of the players who would use this legitimately, but we have recorded cases within web3 in which 1 single individual, through greedy actions, caused the breakdown of years of development and passion for the projects.

author: [Λ] Neo_AArmstrong [EC]Econ Vantarah
content: I'm not advocating for this. Rewards in a f2p model has to be cosmetic only or you'll get exactly what you described. Those cosmetics could have their own value if they're seasonal, one-time, or rare (hard to get). Which is time and rng based. I think one big issue today is that how people earn in SA is very static. FICs with fixed recipes and fixed replenishment. It allows people to establish pretty simple "workflows" that constantly depletes those pools. If they could develop seasonal quest lines that change greatly between seasons (so you don't lock into one meta) and have the community directly part of forming that (Blaseball did this with post season votes.. the developers still had "some control" they created the options to be voted on.. but the community picked their own path). Then you'd constantly need people to trade in time or luck (rng) to make er.  Downside here is that it's more work and who ever wants to do more work for less, amirite?

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content:
I have a different opinion. Crank up ship redemption. Give us alternatives on what to do. More people crafting ships = less emissions on FICs, and less diluted LP.

Does it inflate total mining power? Yes, for the next 3-4 months. Because at C4, fighters get a nerf. And Hogs were never good miners anyway.

Another side effect: once we get more players (hopefully soon), we need supply. Both from ATMTA, but from our own. The whole premise behind SA was that, at some point, the economy would shift from emissions to self sustained.

Ship redemption is a great step towards that.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content:
Give us Armstrong mini-TIP, an XS miner. Let us craft Shipits. Or whatever. Just expand the available recipes. And keep it in line with the model of increasing the recipe cost on each batch.
If anything, that's a R9 sink in itself.

I crafted an embarassing amount of Hogs back in the day, even when the recipe was worse than doing LP. But it kept me engaged, and possibly was what kept me in the project. And I'm not alone in this.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: Guys, is there any forecast for the arrival of the auction system for fleet rentals? Today I came across an account that rented 85 fleets in a 4-second interval. A legitimate player can't even find fleets at competitive prices if bots simply clear the market every time. It's almost ridiculous that we're forced to look for market bots and risk our accounts to try to maintain a minimum of fair play here. I ask that you please pay attention to this, especially considering the new players who will come with Z.ink and will be frustrated with so many bots sniping everything.

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content: In regards to this question, I've been very busy with some quality of life upgrades and a few rabbit holes. It's not the easiest thing to do because of all the moving parts. These are the in flight things (no particular order)   1) ensured that rentals would work with C4 ASAP which also means it now works with Holosim  2) reworked the system for simplicity and flexibility specifically related to rental windows. (you'll be able to rent fleets in hourly increments if you'd like. There's still minimum rental durations and specific payment frequencies) You'll also be able to do every 24hrs vs 0000 UTC (@daily) for payments as an owner.  3) created an SDK to allow for people integrate fleet rentals into their tooling (Eveeye, etc)  4) created a CLI to make testing easier and allow for terminal interactions with the rental system.  5) updated the fees/payment system to allow for referrals in the system (10% of fees, meaning I'll be getting 40% and giving them 10%) 6) Bug fixes and ability for others to "unstick" stuck rentals easier  With all of those landing soon TM the next feature set is what I'm calling a priority queue.

reply.preview: In regards to this question, I've been very busy with some quality of life upgrades and a few rabbit holes. It's not the easiest thing to do because of all the moving parts. (you'll be able to rent fleets in hourly increments if you'd like. There's still minimum rental durations and specific payment frequencies) You'll also be able to do every 24hrs vs 0000 UTC (@daily) for payments as an owner. 3) created an SDK to allow for people integrate fleet rentals into their tooling (Eveeye, etc) 4) created a CLI to make testing easier and allow for terminal interactions with the rental system. 5) updated the fees/payment system to allow for referrals in the system (10% of fees, meaning I'll be getting 40% and giving them 10%) 6) Bug fixes and ability for others to "unstick" stuck rentals easier With all of those landing soon TM the next feature set is what I'm calling a priority queue.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
We're kind of stuck in this position where from a market structure standpoint, the tokens don't look attractive. Liquidity is low, and Tier 1 exchange options are limited. Whales don't want to buy the token because they'd incur too much slippage. Those dynamics make us less attractive to any exchanges because the volume isn't there and the chart looks bad. The result is that any exchange listing would be incredibly expensive, if even possible. And we're not in a position to speculate on exchange listings with an investment from our balance sheet. 

Where we can effect this as a team is via product adoption. Which is where we focus our energy. I genuinely believe in our tokenomics, but the driver of that flywheel is seeing genuine user growth. Then everything kicks in and we can see the effects of the positive feedback loop. But we need to get the players. Which means we need to get product  out that attracts them.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
Part of what we're doing with zink, specifically by including ATLAS and POLIS in the XP earnings mechanics, is intended to drive volume to those tokens. Bring more token holders in. Get more tokens locked. Create more trade volume. All things we can leverage in discussions with exchanges. 

And while I hesitate to even say this publicly, and caveat emptor, highly speculative, there is a possibility that we can leverage a TGE Tier 1 listing of zink to also include ATLAS and POLIS. Most exchanges want what's called a "primary listing." Which is to be the first exchange to list a token on TGE. We obviously don't have that option with ATLAS and POLIS, but we do with ZINK. Secondary listings are exceptionally difficult because you're benchmarked against a variety of metrics that, frankly, don't look good for us right now. We have a new opportunity with zink, and I'll do everything within my power to utilize that to create momentum for A/P too

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
There is an elegant waterfall effect we have the opportunity to create here with zink. This is separate from our product development driving adoption, and therefore driving the flywheel effect of our tokenomics. But it's closely related.

Drive massive hype and interest for zink, this new SVM L1,Provide an airdrop campaign as a method of securing a position in zink,Airdrop campaign funnels users into Star Atlas products and ATLAS and POLIS,Star Atlas flywheel kicks in and drives ATLAS and POLIS buy/hold behavior,ATLAS / POLIS price go up, bringing more speculators into the tokens. Higher earnings (resulting from higher ATLAS price) brings more players in. Drives revenue in asset sales. Lifts the floor of all assets.,We can scale our team significantly if this all succeeds. Ship product of higher quality, more depth, more frequently. Restart the flywheel.,

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I think step 4 is the biggest hurdle. I think Ye Average Gamer is going to check out Holosim and spend some time in UE5. If they aren't crypto native, I think they'll completely ignore the tokens. The pieces of the ecosystem that use the tokens aren't attractive to a gamer audience.  I think the majority of traffic will be from "airdrop farmers". They'll do whatever free activities they can do to get a piece of the airdrop. The only way they transition into buying ships and playing SAGE is if it makes sense from an ROI perspective.  I think the missing piece is more attractive ecosystem-bound gameplay. Maybe "black goo" helps here, but I'm not sure if that hits in time for this campaign. And SAGE drastically needs to be more accessible. If it's a tactical resource manager, we need mobile access and passive operations. If it's a a galactic conquest game, we need responsiveness, some snazzy animations, and detailed tactical information readily available.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: And the way we're structuring/weighting XP mechanics, it will always make more sense to own assets and play in the live economy side versus free mechanics like Holosim. Not to mention, we're adding more and more layers to the economy across Holosim and UE, so there are earnings opportunities beyond the zink airdrop itself. That's the hook.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: For example, if they buy assets for SAGE, they'll earn a considerably higher airdrop, but they'll also be earning ATLAS as they play. Paying for the Battle Pass in Holosim let's them earn a higher airdrop, but also have a better opportunity for the reward structure there. And buying crew for UE let's them also unlock various incentives (crew packs + potentially Goo).

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I haven't heard much about the ATLAS Locker aspect lately, so I'm probably saying stuff that has already been discussed ad nauseam. This sounds like an opportunity to take a page out of the mobile gacha playbook. You unlock the first reward relatively quickly, and for free. The next reward costs a little, and takes a considerable amount of time (2-4 weeks). It scales from there.  Ultimately, the player has the opportunity to experience first hand the value of whatever that reward is. Then they can decide for themselves if they want to spend their money or their time. Or in some cases, the high-end things are just outright paywalled.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: I think step 4 is the biggest hurdle. I think Ye Average Gamer is going to check out Holosim and spend some time in UE5. If they aren't crypto native, I think they'll completely ignore the tokens. The pieces of the ecosystem that use the tokens aren't attractive to a gamer audience.  I think the majority of traffic will be from "airdrop farmers". They'll do whatever free activities they can do to get a piece of the airdrop. The only way they transition into buying ships and playing SAGE is if it makes sense from an ROI perspective.  I think the missing piece is more attractive ecosystem-bound gameplay. Maybe "black goo" helps here, but I'm not sure if that hits in time for this campaign. And SAGE drastically needs to be more accessible. If it's a tactical resource manager, we need mobile access and passive operations. If it's a a galactic conquest game, we need responsiveness, some snazzy animations, and detailed tactical information readily available.

author: defiantwyvern
content:
First of all, I support an L1 chain for Star Atlas (maybe not the only solution though) - there is no doubt that C4 needs a more efficient chain than solana mainnet to thrive. I understand the direction with z.ink, and appreciate what the player profile concept brings to the table. I have several thoughts and concerns to share with the team.

z.ink Subscriptions,Attracting new users,z.ink and its relation to the SA cybernation.,
Although I expect you have discussed many of these topics, please consider them as constructive thoughts

author: defiantwyvern
content:
Regarding the z.ink subscription,
This goes against what I came to expect from ATMTA as a pioneer in Web3. I showed up in web3 for digital ownership, not subscriptions.,How can the subscription be paid? Credit card ($USD), USDC, ATLAS, etc? Mostly just curious on this one.,Asking for subscription payments before z.ink is live doesn't feel right.,Taking into account what products will be available on z.ink at launch, sell me on why z.ink needs a subscription model initially. Without telling me about airdrop XP boost.,Having a subscription boost up front will drive potential new users away. It feels like a gamified ponzi scheme - purchase a subscription to boost XP for a future airdrop of unknown value.,ATMTA/z.ink will be raising capital, getting a large chunk of initial z.ink supply, and will be earning z.ink fees by running validators. Is this not enough to get started? If you have valuable services to offer later, subscriptions can be added down the line without eating into perception at launch.,With the current airdrop plan, you'll be squeezing SA players for subscription fees, who would get the most "value" out of a subscription boost. Obviously, an individual can choose, but it still feels bad to me.,Looking forward, I have concerns bout how a (centralized?) subscription works on a decentralized chain. Probably doesn't matter for a while.,

author: defiantwyvern
content:
Regarding new users (& OGs),
Star Atlas OGs do not need a bailout for being OGs. Build the game we were sold on, that's what we want. If z.ink is an affordable chain at launch, an OG can purchase millions of TX worth of z.ink gas for $5. They already have incentive to use z.ink - star atlas moving there. It's others that need the incentive.

If you really want to attract new players, show them the future without asking for any money. Make them feel that they matter as much or more than the old guard. If they find your products interesting enough, they will stay and purchase assets.

by zXP, below, I'm talking about z.ink XP for airdrop purposes. It's not clear to me if there are other needs/uses for z.ink XP in the short term.

Heavy zXP for playing C4 PTR - This represents a major pillar in the future of Star Atlas, where the majority of future asset value can be consumed by new users.,Heavy zXP for playing UE5 Shooter modes - Another pillar of future Star Atlas, and the bread and butter of making normal gamers feel comfortable to get involved.,No zXP for playing holosim. It's cool, but it also doesn't represent the core values of the Star Atlas metaverse. (right?),Little to no zXP for playing Starbased. Will all the SBs mostly upgraded, with DAU that shrank over time, Starbased is not the product we want to drive people toward right now. Plus, new users stand no chance with modest investments. Compared to what C4 (PTR) could feel like, they will only be disappointed by Starbased.,Little to no zXP for POLIS/ATLAS lockers. These are critical parts of the SA ecosystem, but if the goal is new user acquisition, there is not much point in rewarding these lockers. You'll get more new users if you don't ask them to lock money up for weeks or years.,zXP for primary market purchases seems alright,No subscription to boost zXP.,

author: defiantwyvern
content: I don't particularly want to go deep here right now, and hopefully this doesn't practically matter, so I'll keep 3 short. I understand there are reasons for keeping it separate ... BUT, from the perpsective of a Star Atlas citizen, I expected the L1 for SA should be the backbone of the Star Atlas cybernation, not a separate product. This launch allows z.ink to exist without Star Atlas, but Star Atlas won't be able to exist without z.ink. Perhaps good for ATMTA, not as directly for SA. (Hopefully turns out good for SA ),

author: [COEX] BulutONI
content:
Common sense is universal. The only way for this project to survive is through a strong and stable Atlas and POLIS price. Anyone claiming otherwise is either not taking it seriously or is being dishonest.

So, what are the ways to create a strong Atlas and POLIS price? One of the most obvious methods is to list these tokens on as many exchanges as possible and encourage users on those platforms to buy and hold them. For this to work effectively, exchanges also need to offer staking options.
However, unfortunately, many exchanges demand very high listing fees, so we’ve had to eliminate this strategy for now.
Instead, our goal is to first demonstrate how strong and valuable we are—so that exchanges will come to us and want to list our tokens.

Until now, the team had been sustaining itself by selling Atlas, Polis, ship assets, and crews. However, as the years passed, the continuous devaluation of Atlas and Polis became unsustainable, and a new roadmap was needed.
They had mentioned this need to us from time to time.
Eventually, they reached a decision: since there was no actual game released yet, they focused on alternative solutions—one of which was creating a Layer-1 blockchain.
Some of us called it “Atlasnet,” others called it “Apollo.”
But in the end, they decided the most fitting name was “Z.ink,” and that’s what they chose to call it.

I will continue

author: [COEX] BulutONI
content:
Now we are at the most critical point.

For us, the most important goal is attracting more players to the game. To achieve this, Star Atlas is introducing a reward system. No matter which of their current games you play, you will earn rewards. If you use all available tools together, your earnings will increase. On top of that, they will encourage you to buy a “Battle Pass,” which will further help boost your profits.

This is truly a great idea. With this strategy, they will undoubtedly attract a significant player base. There are millions of people around the world who spend hours—even days—at their computers chasing airdrops, and nearly 80% of them are gaming enthusiasts.

So far, so good.

But what we really need is for the Star Atlas tokens to gain value.

Now, when the Z.ink system is activated, if all activity is limited to Orca and Raydium, people will begin to sell their earnings immediately. This will create a vicious cycle, bringing us back to the original issue—exchange listings and staking. The same problem will apply to Z.ink as well. Because if users earn income in a token that continuously loses value, they may eventually see it as worthless and withdraw from the system entirely.

To prevent this, Z.ink must gain value.

Although it may look attractive at first, the outcome could be disappointing.

If everyone looks back at their past statements, it's clear that everything revolves around money. Players join the game if they can earn something; if not, they won’t. That's exactly what we've been saying, and that's how we've been acting. The current system we're trying to implement reflects the same logic. We’ve even reached a point where we're practically telling players: “You don’t have to bring in revenue — we’ll just give you free rewards.” Our goal is simply to encourage participation.

The biggest issue for current players is the fees paid to the Solana network. We're struggling to attract new players because the game now requires serious capital. Right now, someone who enters with $3,000–4,000 barely earns anything, which prevents them from joining us.

The most important benefit Z.ink will bring us, let’s not forget, is lower transaction fees. And the team must remain consistent on this. If someone can join the system with just $300–500 and earn revenue, they’ll stay — you can count on that.

Another important point is keeping the earned income within the system. The solution here is to expand the staking options. Unfortunately, our current staking system is very weak. Hopefully, you’ll find a solution for that too.

author: defiantwyvern
content: The subscription is completely optional. But I do see it as a novel introduction of a revenue model that you really don't see in crypto today. Whether you subscribe or not, it doesn't impact any of the assets that you own in SA, and wouldn't apply to zink token holdings. The subscription primarily applies to the digital identity layer of zink, but will also be leveraged for the "marketing and distribution" side of the platform. The subscription will initially be payable with credit card (can potentially add token payment options later),Again, totally optional. It's going to have a bunch of perks like XP multiplier, access to a Holosim Battle Pass, .ink naming service, game asset reward track, etc.,See above. Investors will also want to know how this business monetizes.,Some people might perceive it that way. Again, totally optional.,Noting again, investors will want to see how this platform monetizes. Creating cash flows for zink, and also for ATMTA, are all net positive for Star Atlas. Perhaps the most salient point is that it could result in ATMTA being a net buyer of tokens, instead of a net seller.,Ok,Chain functionality, and even the digital identity layer, should be fully supported with or without a subscription. The subscription just adds value. And ideally, adds so much value that everyone wants to use it -- like Amazon Prime. Cash flows are good.,

author: defiantwyvern
content: That's precisely what we're doing. Building the vision we outlined. Can you imagine the response if we didn't facilitate an airdrop to SA players? The accusations that we were just forcing SA players to buy a new token? I can smell the fire from the torches now.  Believe it or not, money is important. This massive vision has a massive budget. And getting to the fulcrum point in adoption is going to require significantly more capital.   zink XP is the status representation of your Avatar on zink, and will have massive implications for those users in the future. Near term, it's primarily centered on the airdrop. Otherwise, I think you're missing key points on the weighting of XP for tracking mechanics.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I'm not accusing forcing current players to buy a new token. (maybe accusing them of being coerced into a subscription, however ) To the contrary, I'm saying it's 100% ok if current players need to purchase z.ink to play since it will be affordable for gameplay needs.  The core of my statement here is that gaining more users should be higher priority than airdropping more % to current SA community. Maybe it is set up that way, really that comes down the how XP stacks up. Funneling new users toward products that they are more likely to be interested in will be more likely to keep them around. And not saying to totally shaft current users - as you state, they are still well positioned to participate because of familiarity with products.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: for the discussion on rentals in ⁠😎┃foundation-room the issue is not actually in the fact that alot of rental fleets are working in SAGE, the main issue is that alot of those rentals are cheap, meaning the threshold for profit margins is ALOT lower, compared to people who had to say, buy a ship on primary, given enough scale (aka bot armies), this could affect the economy as a whole, as everyone has to lower prices if they want to sell something, which is obviously not ideal. Main solution to this would be to allow for rental fleets to be competitively snapped up by legit players, who would gladly do bidding wars against each other to get valuable fleets for their operations.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: And the argument of "a bot will win the bidding war anyway" is moot, because I'd rather a bot rent a fleet for 1k ATLAS/day, instead of renting it for just 200 ATLAS/day (and this is not an exaggeration on the cheapness of some rental fleets).

author: Chri.z [STAR]
content: @Definitely_Not_JayWalk I'm gonna put on my free-market economist hat and say that what you're describing sounds a bit like implementing a quota, which is not something that usually ends well. Effectively, restricting the market causes a deadweight loss (whether it's a price control or a quantity demand control). It sounds like the main issue, once again, comes down to our one-sided market. I think Marketplace V3 where we have best execution will solve this pain point. Bots cause headaches in the commodities markets too. What are your thoughts?

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content: From a (definitely) not economic point of view, I think rental bots are killing the chaos you can have inside the game: they play a deterministic game and there is not inefficiency in doing that (while a player can have inefficiency). The only way to peacefully kill the bots is to prevent deterministic actions from the game or add a quite decent(and not bottable) layer of maintenance

author: Chri.z [STAR]
content: Great thoughts, Chris. What are your views about an unregulated free market?  Without some controls on abuses by large actors (i.e. monopolies), would free market principles still hold to be effective?  It seems that we are experimenting with the right balance of regulation (your role) and market participation (DAU). The more you regulate against abuses and manipulation, the higher the DAU may become. Am I wrong?

author: Chri.z [STAR]
content: I guess my rebuttal would be that a free market is not the same as a "free for all" market where anything goes.  Even in a free market there are rules and regulations put in place such as anti-monopoly regulations, fair trade acts and small business protections...all which help aid in economic stability. We essentially have none of those protections here.  Imagine if Ford could ultimately mine all their iron, produce all their steel, manufacture all their chips, farm all their rubber and textiles...they would eliminate thousands of small businesses.  What we are allowing here is essentially the same thing. We are handicapping a real economic society by not putting some guardrails in place.   I am 100% for as free of a market as we can get, but you also have to understand that we get their over time and with wider broader markets. You can't just set people free and expect efficient markets without some help at first getting their.  edit: Earther stole my thunder as I was typing.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: I think you're hitting on something really important (lol I sound like chatgpt). Monopolies are probably one of the single biggest pain-points free-marketers face "OK but how do yoou deal with Monopolies" then we end up with a bunch of anti-trust law that gets enforced some places and not others. There are some hardcore people that say monopolies wouldn't be able to sustain for long if it weren't for governments coming in and bailing them out (think banks and airlines). I honestly find this argument compelling. The issue is, what's the cost of a monopoly failing and is it worth it to "let the market run". Back to the point you made:  Yeah, I think massive players definitely pose a very important risk to our ecosystem. I've heard the term Leviathon used before and Ithink it's apt. There are players that are simply a different species. They play a different game with different rules due to their size. My goal has always been to try and reach a point where thye can play their game and have fun and everyone else can play their game and have fun. Without too much interference. Now, saying it and doing it are two very different things. I think the FIC issue doesn't really back me up on my mission statement unfortunately.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
Capitalism without revolt is tyranny dressed as prosperity: it fattens the powerful while teaching the powerless to cheer their own servitude. Once, rebellion was the tax on greed, the reminder that hunger sharpens into justice when ignored too long. Now the poor are disarmed, lulled with distractions and debts, and greed grows unchecked, no longer fearing the guillotine or the barricade. A society that forgets rebellion does not advance, it decays, for nothing remains to humble excess, and nothing is more dangerous than power that no longer trembles.

Tl;Dr wen combat

author: ROME | Earther [ROME]
content: What is ultimately important for me is that we do not place an upper bound on the desire to make a capital investment into assets because of these restrictions. People earning from the game and reinvesting those earnings into more assets is a strong net positive for every asset holder. If we restrict the ability to ultimately scale, it reduces total demand for game assets. And ultimately (likely) leads to multi-accounting anyway.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Now, someone that progresses significantly in the game would presumably be able to take significant advantage of rentals. But there's still kind of a soft ceiling on the quantity they can control (based on CR). So then it largely becomes a question of whether or not they should purchase those fleets instead of renting them regularly. ROI calculation. If things go to plan, the long term ROI on ownership will be superior.

If you allow a person to rent 4X (or even up to 10X) their VWAP you are still giving them massive earnings potential to play the zero to hero role.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: There's a great youtube video about how to mathematically detect/catch a cheater. Which this boils down too. When you add limits or restrictions or "gates" they also affect everyone not just one group. As such, make it too difficult you'll not have the bots sure but you'll also miss out on the casuals (new players). I've been noodling a priority queue instead of an auction. I don't really want to lock peoples funds up while they wait for a rental that may or may not be there when they end up using it. To prevent this there is a 24 hour claim window in the priority queue implementation. I feel it's fine though since it's related to "high valued" rentals but doesn't affect rentals without a queue (same mechanics today).

author: Matteo985xxxMUD
content: You can't accurately compare unfinished projects.  Its like comparing the value of a nearly finished house to an apartment building that is just a foundation and materials.  People can understand the utility/value of the house.  Few want to buy, or are capable of valuing a complex project before its done.  Once complete and functional however, the apartment building is worth 20x the house. Star Atlas will turn the corner soon, and people will understand what is being built.  Until then, there is no reason for its valuation to make any sense.

author: [Λ] Neo_AArmstrong [EC]Econ Vantarah
content:
@Xcode Could you please check the marketplace sales exploit currently occurring in Holosim?
There are players exploiting this and progressing in minutes where it should have taken hours.
The exploit is simple: The quest that should only consider selling to the NPC wallet is actually considering selling to any wallet.
If I create a secondary account, sell 10 Food/Metal/Plastic Contracts for 0.000000001, and then buy them again, I can keep looping and complete the quest that should take 13-14 hours in 5-10 minutes, giving an unfair and undue advantage, almost like an "XP boost."
If the blacklist is going to apply to players who exploit XP in UE5, it should also apply to all exploiters who used it in Holosim.

author: [Λ] Neo_AArmstrong [EC]Econ Vantarah
content: If no one has completed this mission, even better, because this exploit needs to be fixed quickly to prevent hundreds of players from abusing it. I've even seen streamers discover that selling the Water contract in CSS already works in the tutorial mission, so connecting the dots and realizing that it works in this more complex one will be a serious problem.

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content: This one is big, need to be fixed

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: I agree it totally needs to be fixed just wondering what the consequences are.

author: [Λ] Neo_AArmstrong [EC]Econ Vantarah
content:
I tested selling 1 contract here and it actually advanced the quest progression, so it is actually applying to the quest count. 
Maybe everyone on the leaderboard is fine because they are veteran players and have been playing since the first season, but players from other communities arriving and hunting for exploits will find this in less than 24 hours, without a doubt.

author: The Man | RomeONI
content:
@SW4GN3R 
With the holosim battle pass we may be setting ourselves up for a pay to win fallout. Too late to change it for this round probably, but if the incentives are tied to XP amounts, and the battle pass give bonus XP....
 I was under the impression that Holosim was intended to get new players in. Having a clear pay to win advantage in the onboarding process is likely going to scare people off, most gamers seem to have extremely low opinions of pay to win. Alternatively, if someone wants to try and earn $50 USD because it's a large amount where they are, then they likely don't want to have to front $10 because that's also a lot. But then if they will be at a large handicap against the people who did buy it, relegating them to leftovers why invest the time?

Napkin math, if we have 300 people buy a battle pass, that's $3,000 a month/season.  If $600,000 isn't enough to matter much for runway I feel like we are squeezing blood from the wrong stone. That's 200 months to get to what has been called not much, or over 16 years of 300 monthly battle passes.

author: The Man | RomeONI
content: A bunch of comments here since this post. From my perspective, I want to first clarify that both the team (quite aggressively), and I want to avoid the perception of P2W. Generally, so far the feedback has been largely positive and has not triggered this response that you're feeling. That's largely validated by the people responding to your message. The thing with Holosim is that cosmetic reward tracks aren't really enticing given the fidelity of the game. So we needed something that actually feels valuable. But we/I don't feel like this is outright P2W. As others have commented, there is every opportunity to play the game and have fun without it. But the BP does provide the best opportunity to place in the reward tracks. If you want to extract value from the game, then you kind of should have to put some value in. Otherwise, just play and have fun. There's nothing other players can do that you can't just because they buy the BP.  With that said, we're not fixated on these being the perks in perpetuity. This is more of an experiment. Brand new revenue model for us. And while the revenue from the BP so far is low relative to what we need to see in recurring cash flow for it to be considered "successful," what is encouraging is the participation rate -- floating around 22%. These are insights we can learn from and utilize to drive strategy in the future.  To be clear, I think Holosim has breakout potential, and could achieve DAU greater than 100,000. If the BP model works at scale, we're looking at revenue figures that do start to move the needle for us. The product goals for Holosim include:  Diversified player base with potential to attract a web2 audience,Potential to convert some of those players into mainnet users, I don't expect high conversion rates into SAGE, at least not presently. But having a new player base and consistent recurring revenue stream from the product would be a big win for the studio.

author: [SAI] Torinder ⪛๏⫺ [SKR]
content: All you need to do is to find out the token addresses of the new Holosim game ID and replace the ones from Sage in SLYA's code. And because Holosim is quite competitive, it is possible that some players already made a new modified SLYA version for Holosim without publishing it. If you have a head start of 1 or 2 days, you will definitely be a top player. So they would be putting themselves at a disadvantage if they made the version public. Yeah, it is sad, because SLYA was always meant to be open source, but we can't do anything about it.

author: [SLY] Groove [STAR]
content:
RE: the cloud service discussion...

Personally, I'd like to see a game design that doesn't rely on this sort of continual repetitive instruction. Setting aside the back-end logistics and how you'd get it done, what is the intended gameplay? If there are no new decisions that need to be made, there shouldn't be any interaction required. It's the equivalent of assigning your worker to a task in any sim-type game.

I think it either needs to be sped way up so that mining is an active activity similar to EVE, or treated as a purely passive activity similar to assigning a worker a task in Star Craft.

Now, aside from that - the biggest problem I have with the current services that are available is that it encourages poor security practices. Yes, I understand that we're using proxy wallets, but it's still just bad opsec. It's never a good idea to normalize poor security habits.

The other issue I have is that we're superficially injecting a reliance on web2 services to make our web3 game work as we want it to.

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
Imho, automation is necessary as long as you’re playing an RTS-style game. The more the game shifts from RTS to MMORPG, the more automation (as we currently know it) will disappear — especially because UE5 doesn’t allow third-party software.

I don’t know how long SAGE will last, but I assume that once SAGE is sunset, automation will die with it.

For the time being, the only way to address this is by allowing Claim Stakes to farm constantly without any “human” intervention — basically a set-and-forget system until you need the resources it has mined, then you redeem the resources. This won’t solve the root issue, but it will help most players (only miner ships would still require automation at that point).

Personally, I’m very much against automating combat in any form — and luckily, it won’t be possible to a large extent.

Crafting could be automated with some sort of queue system, but that’s not really game-changing.

author: Icy
content:
can we stop pretending sage is a RTS game?
RTS vs LS — the clean split,What an RTS is (by definition),Human, real-time decision-making. The player observes, prioritizes, and adapts on the fly.,Uncertainty & scouting. Fog-of-war or imperfect info forces judgment, not scripts.,Trade-offs under time pressure. Macro vs micro, tech vs army, defend vs expand.,Skill expression. Success comes from reading the game, not repeating a script.,
Classic benchmarks: StarCraft, Age of Empires, Warcraft, Command & Conquer.

What “Looped Strategy” (LS) is,Automation-first. A bot or macro executes a prebuilt loop (gather → build → attack → repeat).,Deterministic rhythm. Actions fire on schedules, not because the game state demands them.,Reactive only within rules. At best, simple if/then heuristics; no genuine planning.,Set-and-forget. Works even if the human walks away.,
Think idle/incremental patterns or botting scripts: consistent, tireless, predictable.

author: Icy
content:
Fast “smell tests”,Ask these and be strict:

1) Would a strong player beat it by surprising it?
   RTS: yes; surprises matter.LS: no; it plows ahead unless explicitly coded.,,
2) Does progress halt if the human stops deciding?
   RTS: quickly.LS: not really.,,
3) Are actions triggered by the current game state or by a timer?
   RTS: state-driven (scouting, threats, timings).LS: timer/sequence-driven.,,
4) Does information asymmetry change behavior?
   RTS: scouting changes everything.LS: minimal change unless prewritten.,,
5) Is high APM necessary and meaningful?
   RTS: yes; inputs map to intent.LS: APM can be inhuman and empty.,,

Holosim is a good step but still if you look at any 4X multiplayer game there is basically a huge learning curve to overcome, the present situation is kinda bad for bringing in new players, i can't imagine anyone who doesn't already have trusted contacts within the star atlas ecosystem putting up thousands of bucks like the OGs did. Things should get much simpler, especially with game aspects like automation tools and so on, cause the experience of new players right now does not look very welcoming to me

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content:
Exactly. Things will become simpler. Hence the new folks will have an easier time to get onboarded than we had.

Like a good boardgame, the goal is to become “easy to learn, difficult to master”.

We are not at “easy to learn” just yet, and many more game mechanics will be added that will make getting there more complex.

But an effort will be made, and things will definitely improve.

A good chunk of our experience and learnings will become irrelevant over time.

author: defiantwyvern
content: Well, it's mostly cosmetic in the sense that these aren't revenue producing assets. There are some other benefits like the crafting bench. But they aren't the same as buying ships or claim stakes. The core concept is about owning a personal slice of real estate in the metaverse.  We don't have all of the mechanics fleshed out for how parcel selection will work. But the original concept was that an auction would start when someone chooses a parcel. And then anyone with priority access can bid on it. Eventually new land plots would be open for anyone to bid on, assuming they own a CSS Plot asset. Entirely possible that the auction ends with zero other bidders and you can secure your location at no added cost. But otherwise we need to way to determine land selection that isn't purely FCFS

reply.preview: Well, it's mostly cosmetic in the sense that these aren't revenue producing assets. There are some other benefits like the crafting bench. author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
Guys. Atlas/Polis lockers for Z.ink rewards are very simple.  They will check for PVP/Atlas deposited amounts at a specific time and date. It does not matter if you deposit/lock now or later. All that matters is you have amounts deposited in the specific lockers when they do the CHECK, at a specific date and time.

tl;dr If you want to deposit now,  that's fine. If you want to deposit closer to the start date, that's fine too.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
@[SAI] Torinder ⪛๏⫺ Faction sniping is not a thing anymore. It would be crazy tedious to move crew and ships from 1 faction to another in any significant quantities to make a difference.  You would do far more damage to yourself by losing the extra time.  If someone wanted to swap factions, sure. But doing that repeatedly is a no go.  It doesn't make any economic sense and even if someone wanted to sabotage it doesn't really work either. 

One option I could see is moving to another faction because they are better at crafting the best LP generating item, e.g. power sources.  But if everyone does that then the LP pool goes up. So not really worth moving.

It's pretty self policing imo.

author: The Man | RomeONI
content:
1- could also be fixed by changing how the game works to not be so input intensive. An unlikely change, but it is currently setup in a way that needs lots of interaction, but Is also as a wait around game. 
You could just as easily set that a unit is mining a resource, and have it auto mine without needing to stop, dock, drop cargo, load cargo, undock, start mining again. Have it in mining mode, and resources from the planet are used slowly by the mining ship, while the mined resource slowly accrues in the base. No further interaction needed until you want to change something. Doesn't even require the browser to be open. 
Could have transport ships slowly trade between two points automatically, not fly, dock, drop cargo, load cargo, undock, fly, dock, drop cargo, load cargo, undock. Just passively move resources between the bases at a fixed rate per second set by the cargo size and speed of the ship that would be equivalent to having it do laps currently, and have it be attackable anywhere along the route.
Again, not likely or expected, but the automation issues could be completely avoided by design. And changed later when this is no longer so painfully incomplete.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: I understand. What I'm saying is that you are asking for a batch transaction operation, which is what I expect Star Frame was designed for.  You want a ship in Star Atlas to operate like how mining in Star Craft happens, it just mines until there is nothing left to mine.  What I'm trying to say is that the Star Atlas team likely had to build two things in order for this to happen in Star Atlas. First you have to create each individual action that a ship needs to do on-chain, then the team needed to build a way to batch this transaction to create efficiency and a better experience for players. It just takes time.  Danny specifically mentioned I think 1.5 years ago that the team would be working towards making operations feel exactly how you described. It's just very likely that the team realized that the tech had not been built to do such an operation.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: Honestly, being a huge web3 maxi. That ATLAS mint is tied to only work being done for the ecosystem and not made up "sinks" out of thin air. Think validators, oracles, attestors, game servers etc. The ingame economy is truly p2p - some people will scan, others mine, some transport, some craft, all working together playing a game. Where the currency is time. I'll spend my time to mine so that you can save your time not doing it because you buy the resources from me. Value is never created out of nowhere. With two different layers the macro (SAGE - logistics and fleet management) and micro (UE5 - flying one ship around doing ship things). Where if in the macro layer a whale has 100 ships transporting resources and you're in the micro you literally see those 100 ships transporting. You can decide to diddle them or help them but it's you interacting at a micro level on the macro. I know a wild dream

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Honestly, being a huge web3 maxi. That ATLAS mint is tied to only work being done for the ecosystem and not made up "sinks" out of thin air. Think validators, oracles, attestors, game servers etc. The ingame economy is truly p2p - some people will scan, others mine, some transport, some craft, all working together playing a game. Where the currency is time. I'll spend my time to mine so that you can save your time not doing it because you buy the resources from me. Value is never created out of nowhere. With two different layers the macro (SAGE - logistics and fleet management) and micro (UE5 - flying one ship around doing ship things). Where if in the macro layer a whale has 100 ships transporting resources and you're in the micro you literally see those 100 ships transporting. You can decide to diddle them or help them but it's you interacting at a micro level on the macro. I know a wild dream

author: Definitely_Not_JayWalk
content: For me, the biggest draw of Holosim over SAGE is that I can just play the game and not worry about how much money it's costing me. Couple that with the multiplied speed and tangible progression, it can be a lot of fun.  However, playing Holosim makes me worried for SAGE. The things that I'm having fun with in Holosim (building advanced infrastructure, trying to build a combat fleet, progressing through levels and ship tiers) simply won't be accessible to me in SAGE. Ever.  I'm working with like a $50k VWAP fleet in Holosim, and it's only now that I really feel like I've got my feet under me and can start producing some excess goods.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Hopefully C4 combat is much more fleshed out than what we see in Holosim.  The Safe Zone is the only area where you can park your fleet if you're away. So when you want to jump in and have that short burst of combat, you have to fly your fleets out there first.  Even if you have your supply chain rock solid and can refuel at your perimeter, it's still like 30 mins minimum to warp all the way to your target and back.  It's not horrible, but it's a lot of watching cooldown timers because if you navigate away to check on something else, Rambo will get you.  And yes, it's always Rambo. Even if your Ustur. Even if you are Rambo. It's still Rambo.

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content:
You can access any system of a Neutral or Border Region. It's only Safe Regions that can not be entered or attacked.
The warp lanes function as soon as your faction controls both ends of it. These only speed up travel, they play no role in determining access to a system or region directly. They do determine which Regions are neighboring each other, but whether they are active (faction owns both ends) or inactive (no one faction owns both ends) does not matter.
